sections.paragraph: (in prose always with art. הַלְּבָנֹון 1 Ki. 5:20 , 23 1Ki 5:23 Ezr 3:7 poet. without art. Psa 29:6 Isa 14:8 29:17 compare Lehrg. p. 656), pr.n. Mount Lebanon (Gr. Λίβανος), on the borders of Syria and Palestine, consisting of two very high ridges, of which the western is called Lebanon, Libanus κατʼ ἐξοχήν: the eastern ridge is partly covered with perpetual snow ( Jer 18:14, whence its Hebrew name לְבָנֹון, Ch. טנּר תַּלְגָא, Arab. جبال الثلج the mountain of snow, compare Alpes), this is called Antilibanus, and towards the south, in Hebr. חֶרְמֹון, which see. The modern name of the valley between Lebanon and Anti-Lebanon is بقعة Bekaa, but it is, however, altogether a different place from בִּקְעַת הַלְּבָנֹון the valley of Lebanon, Jos 11:17 12:7 , as to which see page 136, B . See Relandi Palæstina, t. i. 311; Oedmann, Verm. Sammlungen, fasc. ii. No. 9; Burckhardt’s Travels in Syr. p. 1, seq.; p.214, seq.; Rosenm. Bibl. Alterthumsk. i. 2, p. 236, seq.
